groundHandlers.notes: IAM Local 914 represents roughly 2,000 United ramp service workers at EWR (iamlocal1731.org). United Ground Express, United's wholly owned handling subsidiary, has also staffed EWR per 32BJ SEIU releases.

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.notes: Swissport lists an EWR station (swissport.com network page). Swissport USA cargo workers at EWR struck in Dec 2022 per Newsweek, confirming cargo operations.

groundHandlers.name: PrimeFlight Aviation Services
groundHandlers.role: Third-party handler, ramp, cabin and cargo services
groundHandlers.notes: Active EWR hiring and employee reviews (Indeed). PrimeFlight acquired GAT, an AeroVect partner, in Feb 2026, giving AeroVect a potential warm path onto this ramp.

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.notes: Menzies lists a Newark Liberty (EWR) station on its network site.

tailwinds.headline: Port Authority tests self-driving electric shuttles at EWR with Oceaneering, Ohmio and Glydways
tailwinds.relevance: The airport operator is actively piloting autonomous EVs in a restricted area of EWR to connect facilities to the new AirTrain, demonstrating institutional openness to autonomous vehicles on airport property, a precedent that lowers stakeholder friction for autonomous GSE.

tailwinds.headline: Port Authority board approves annual airport minimum wage increases reaching $25/hr by 2032 at EWR, JFK and LGA
tailwinds.relevance: Policy-mandated ramp labor cost escalation (CPI-tied increases from Jan 2025, floor of $25/hr by 2032) strengthens the ROI case for autonomous baggage and cargo tractors for both handlers and United.

tailwinds.headline: Port Authority approves first phase of $200M Terminal B upgrade program, including baggage handling system refurbishment
tailwinds.relevance: A three-year modernization program touching baggage systems and boarding bridges, plus a planned new Terminal B in the mid-2030s, creates procurement and redesign windows where autonomous GSE can be specified in.

operationsNotes: EWR is United's East Coast hub with a heavily unionized ramp (IAM at United, 32BJ SEIU among contractor workforces). Airport is in a major modernization cycle: $3.5B AirTrain replacement broke ground Oct 7, 2025 (opening 2030), a $200M Terminal B interim upgrade including baggage handling system refurbishment was approved May 2026, and a new Terminal B is planned for the mid-2030s. Note: FAA imposed flight caps at EWR (34 arrivals/departures per hour, June-Oct 2025) due to the air traffic controller shortage, constraining near-term flight volumes (AccuWeather).
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor found deployed or trialing on the EWR ramp (searches for AeroVect, TractEasy/EasyMile, Aurrigo, UISEE at EWR returned nothing). Attractive signals: PrimeFlight (new owner of AeroVect partner GAT since Feb 2026) already operates at EWR; the Port Authority is itself piloting autonomous electric shuttles on airport property in spring 2026, indicating operator openness to autonomy; and PA-mandated wage escalation to $25/hr locks in rising ramp labor costs. Constraint: United self-handles the majority of the ramp, so capturing most EWR volume requires a carrier-led sale to United rather than a handler-led one.

name: Phoenix Sky Harbor International Airport

groundHandlers.name: American Airlines (self-handling)
groundHandlers.notes: PHX is an AA hub; AA hires its own ramp/fleet service agents there (jobs.aa.com Phoenix operations listings).

groundHandlers.name: Unifi
groundHandlers.notes: Full ground handling for Frontier at PHX since June 2022, 17th Frontier-Unifi station (unifiservice.com).

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.notes: At PHX since 1985; runs a cargo warehouse plus ramp/passenger/fueling services (swissport.com PHX page).

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.notes: Active PHX station; ground handling, cargo and into-plane fueling (menziesaviation.com/contacts/usa-phx).

tailwinds.headline: Construction begins on $356M Terminal 3 North 2 Concourse, six gates plus T3-T4 connector, completion 2027
tailwinds.relevance: More gates and flights increase ramp vehicle traffic and baggage tow demand, expanding the addressable tug fleet.

tailwinds.headline: Phoenix City Council approves heat ordinance changes for airport workers
tailwinds.relevance: Rising heat-safety compliance costs and hazardous outdoor conditions strengthen the ROI case for driverless tractors.

tailwinds.headline: New West Terminal accelerated from 2036 to 2033 opening after FAA accepts existing environmental review
tailwinds.relevance: Airport says it is running out of room for airline growth; long-run capacity expansion means sustained GSE fleet growth and new-build infrastructure open to automation.

operationsNotes: Ramp/tarmac workers filed heat-safety complaints (ground temps reported near 162F); Phoenix City Council opened an investigation and inspections found zero working AC units in 12 Sky Chefs catering trucks (fox10phoenix.com). City Council approved heat ordinance amendments for airport workers on 7/3/2025 (azfamily.com). Airport service and concessions workers struck repeatedly in 2023-2024 (kjzz.org). Strikes found were concessions/catering rather than ramp-specific, but heat complaints explicitly include tarmac employees working up to 16-hour shifts (washingtonpost.com 9/6/2023).
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor found on the PHX ramp (searched TractEasy/EasyMile, Aurrigo, UISEE and generic autonomous tug terms). Strong retrofit fit: roughly 90% of bag tugs already electric per sustainability reporting, extreme-heat labor pressure with a new city heat ordinance raising human-labor compliance costs, and a mixed ramp (AA hub self-handling plus Unifi, Menzies, Swissport). Phoenix is also the most AV-friendly US metro; Waymo has run rider-only service to Sky Harbor curbside since 2022-2023, suggesting a receptive airport authority. Wedge candidates: American Airlines hub ops and Unifi (Frontier contract).

name: Mexico City Benito Juárez International Airport
owner: Grupo Aeroportuario de la Ciudad de México
aerovectNotes: Latin America’s 2nd-busiest; nearshoring cargo tailwind.

name: Kuala Lumpur International Airport

groundHandlers.name: AeroDarat Services
groundHandlers.role: Ramp and baggage handler, subsidiary of Malaysia Aviation Group; handles Malaysia Airlines
groundHandlers.notes: Airline-owned handling arm within MAG aviation services portfolio (https://malaysiaaviationgroup.com.my/en/our-brands-and-services/aviation-services/aerodarat-services.html)

groundHandlers.name: Ground Team Red (GTR)
groundHandlers.role: Ramp and baggage handler at KLIA T1 and T2; AirAsia-SATS joint venture
groundHandlers.notes: Launched first 20 of a planned 60 EV baggage tractors at KLIA Oct 10, 2024, described as Malaysia's largest aviation EV deployment (https://newsroom.airasia.com/news/gtr-launches-electric-baggage-tractors-for-use-at-klia-terminals-1-and-2-advancing-sustainable-aviation-in-malaysia). SATS is a JV shareholder and has deployed UISEE autonomy at Changi, a plausible future channel into KUL

groundHandlers.name: Pos Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Independent ground handler at KLIA T1 and T2
groundHandlers.notes: Ran a one-month EV baggage tractor proof of concept Nov-Dec 2023 to evaluate replacing its 60+ diesel tractor fleet (https://www.internationalairportreview.com/news/190546/pos-aviation-takes-a-bold-step-towards-sustainability-ev-trial-at-klia-terminal-1-2/)

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation Malaysia
groundHandlers.role: New entrant: passenger, ramp and cargo services
groundHandlers.notes: JV with MMAG Holdings; began operations Jan 2025 under a 12-month license, first Menzies market entry in Malaysia (https://menziesaviation.com/news/menzies-aviation-secures-ground-handling-license-in-malaysia/)

tailwinds.headline: GTR launches first 20 of 60 planned EV baggage tractors at KLIA T1 and T2
tailwinds.relevance: Active baggage-tractor fleet modernization shows handler capex appetite and creates a retrofit-timing window for autonomy on new electric tractors

tailwinds.headline: MAHB unveils RM11 billion five-year capex plan incl. KLIA expansion, baggage system upgrades and technology modernization
tailwinds.relevance: Post-privatization (GIP/Khazanah/EPF/ADIA) investment program with a KLIA plan targeting 100M+ annual passengers funds ground-ops technology and raises throughput pressure on the ramp

tailwinds.headline: Transport ministry orders overhaul of KLIA baggage handling after April 2026 failure; MAHB directed to boost manpower and report weekly
tailwinds.relevance: Cabinet-level scrutiny of baggage reliability plus an explicit manpower gap strengthens the case for autonomous baggage tractors as resilience and staffing relief

operationsNotes: GSE at KUL is handler_led: handling companies procure and operate their own tractor fleets (GTR's 60-unit EV rollout, Pos Aviation's 60+ diesel tractors), though the two largest handlers are airline-affiliated (AeroDarat under Malaysia Aviation Group; GTR an AirAsia-SATS JV). Labor signal: after the April 2026 T1 baggage failure (1,061 bags left behind, 120 unaccounted), the Cabinet directed MAHB to boost manpower at critical areas and submit weekly reports, and the transport ministry ordered a full overhaul of baggage handling protocols and infrastructure, citing coordination and response-time gaps (https://www.freemalaysiatoday.com/category/nation/2026/04/20/ministry-orders-overhaul-of-klias-baggage-handling-system; https://www.malaymail.com/news/malaysia/2026/04/22/klia-baggage-fiasco-mahb-to-boost-manpower-submit-weekly-reports-after-cabinet-review/217280). No strikes found; the trigger was a power failure, but the manpower directive signals staffing strain. No autonomous-GSE trial or deployment at KUL found in public sources.
aerovectNotes: Open ramp with no autonomy vendor present. Attractive: handlers are mid-fleet-turnover to electric baggage tractors (GTR 60-unit program, Pos evaluating replacement of 60+ diesels), which is exactly the retrofit window AeroVect targets; Cabinet-level pressure to fix baggage reliability plus MAHB's RM11B post-privatization capex (tech upgrades, KLIA expansion past 100M pax) create budget and political cover for automation. Watch item: SATS holds 50% of GTR and already runs UISEE at Changi, so UISEE has a credible channel into KUL via GTR; Menzies (new entrant Jan 2025) is a global-account door. New GTR/Pos EV tractors may not be AeroVect-retrofit-compatible models; verify platform fit early.

name: King Abdulaziz International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Saudi Ground Services (SGS)
groundHandlers.role: Dominant national handler; ramp, baggage, passenger services at 28+ Saudi airports with Jeddah as a main station
groundHandlers.notes: Holds SAR 5.9B Saudia contract (2021-2026, renewable), SAR 2B flynas renewal (2024-2028), and Riyadh Air contracts (SAR 1.5B from Sep 2024 plus SAR 500M from Mar 2025). Owns a 50% stake in TLD Arabia JV with TLD Group for GSE maintenance and automation services, which is a note of caution since TLD co-owns TractEasy.

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.role: Second handler; at JED since 2016, expanded to 13 Saudi airports after April 24, 2025 GACA license, launched Air Arabia handling June 1, 2025
groundHandlers.notes: Deployed its own GSE fleets to the new stations, indicating handler-owned equipment.

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation (via SAL MoU)
groundHandlers.role: MoU with SAL Saudi Logistics Services for LCC passenger handling at Saudi airports; ramp presence at JED not confirmed
groundHandlers.notes: Signal of continued GACA ground handling liberalization rather than confirmed JED ramp operations.

tailwinds.headline: Jedco plans SR115bn ($31bn) expansion of King Abdulaziz International to 114 million passengers, with fourth runway from 2025 and Terminal 2 from 2026
tailwinds.relevance: Massive airside buildout and long-haul capacity growth multiplies baggage/cargo tow demand and creates a greenfield window to design autonomy into ramp operations.

tailwinds.headline: Swissport scales up in Saudi Arabia with 13-airport launch after April 24, 2025 GACA license, including existing Jeddah operation
tailwinds.relevance: Ground handling liberalization puts a global AeroVect-relevant handler in growth mode at JED with its own GSE fleet, giving a retrofit vendor both a competitive wedge and an existing-account entry path.

tailwinds.headline: King Abdulaziz International sets record with more than 50 million passengers in 2025 (53.4M)
tailwinds.relevance: Record and still-growing throughput, with sharp Hajj peaks, strains ramp capacity and strengthens the case for autonomous tow capacity that scales without seasonal hiring.

operationsNotes: JED is Saudia's hub and the primary Hajj/Umrah gateway with a dedicated Hajj terminal; handled a record 53.4M passengers in 2025 (travelandtourworld.com). Jeddah Airports Company (Jedco) is running a SR115B ($31B) masterplan to reach 114M annual passengers: fourth runway 2025-2029, new Terminal 2 from 2026 to 2031, plus a 15M-pax Hajj/Umrah terminal (meed.com). GSE is handler_led: SGS and Swissport own and operate their fleets (Swissport shipped full GSE fleets to new stations in its June 2025 launch; SGS runs GSE maintenance through its TLD Arabia JV). GACA has been liberalizing ground handling licenses (Swissport April 2025, SAL, Jetex), ending the near-monopoly structure.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE trial or deployment found at JED in searches (TractEasy, Aurrigo, EasyMile, UISEE checks all came back empty for Jeddah; nearest activity is EasyMile shuttles at KAUST and planned autonomous airside vehicles at NEOM Bay). Ramp is open and attractive: record 53.4M pax in 2025, $31B expansion to 114M capacity, extreme Hajj/Umrah seasonal peaks that suit elastic autonomous capacity, and fresh multi-handler competition (Swissport 13-airport expansion) that gives challengers a reason to differentiate. Channel risk: SGS holds 50% of TLD Arabia (JV with TLD Group), and TLD co-owns TractEasy, so the incumbent handler has a pre-built route to the main purpose-built competitor. Entry paths: Swissport global account (already an AeroVect-relevant handler segment) or SGS directly as fleet owner.

name: George Bush Intercontinental Airport

groundHandlers.name: United Airlines (self-handling)
groundHandlers.role: Dominant hub carrier, self-handles its own ramp
groundHandlers.notes: IAH is a flagship United hub with 14,000+ Houston employees; United operates 1,800+ GSE vehicles there including 1,100+ electric units and 15+ Super Tugs. Source: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/united-airlines-breaks-ground-on-new-177m-ground-service-equipment-facility-and-opens-new-state-of-the-art-technical-operations-training-facility-at-george-bush-intercontinental-airport-302408182.html

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.notes: Lists IAH as an active station; has served Houston airlines since 1995. Source: https://www.swissport.com/en/network/north-america/united-states/iah

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Third-party ground handler and fuel farm operator
groundHandlers.notes: Offers ground services at IAH and holds a long-term contract to manage the IAH fuel farm and hydrant fueling system. Sources: https://menziesaviation.com/our-network/george-bush-intercontinental-iah/ and https://menziesaviation.com/news/menzies-aviation-appointed-to-manage-iah-fuel-farm-in-houston-texas/

groundHandlers.name: dnata
groundHandlers.notes: Entered the US market at IAH via the 2017 AirLogistix USA acquisition; operates a 30,000 sq ft cargo/perishables facility. dnata is a global AeroVect partner. Source: https://www.dnata.com/media-centre/dnata-commences-cargo-operations-in-the-usa-with-investments-in-houston-and-dallas/

tailwinds.headline: United breaks ground on $177M, 140,000 sq ft GSE maintenance facility at IAH supporting 1,800+ ground vehicles incl. 1,100+ eGSE
tailwinds.relevance: Major GSE fleet modernization and electrification investment; a carrier upgrading its GSE ecosystem is a natural entry point for retrofit autonomy on baggage/cargo tractors.

tailwinds.headline: $2.5B IAH Terminal B Transformation progressing: 40 gates, automated baggage handling system, capacity for 36M annual passengers, opening fall 2026
tailwinds.relevance: Capacity growth plus an automated baggage system signals openness to ramp automation and will increase bag-tow volumes United must staff or automate.

tailwinds.headline: PrimeFlight Aviation Services (HQ Sugar Land, TX) acquires AeroVect partner GAT Airline Ground Support, creating a 250+ airport handler
tailwinds.relevance: AeroVect's US deployment partner is now owned by a Houston-area headquartered handler, a potential channel into the Houston market; GAT/PrimeFlight presence on the IAH ramp itself is unverified.

operationsNotes: Mixed GSE model: United self-handles its dominant hub operation with its own 1,800+ vehicle GSE fleet (1,100+ electric), while third parties (Swissport, Menzies, dnata cargo) handle other carriers. Labor: United's IAM fleet service (ramp) workers rejected the 2026 tentative agreement system-wide, keeping ramp contract tension open (https://viewfromthewing.com/united-airlines-baggage-and-check-in-workers-reject-contract-as-members-rebel-against-their-union/); the late-2025 government shutdown caused repeated ground stops and severe staffing strain at IAH, though on the ATC/TSA side rather than the ramp (https://www.houstonpublicmedia.org/articles/news/transportation/airports/2025/10/24/534260/ground-stops-houston-airports-air-traffic-controllers-government-shutdown/). No ramp strike at IAH found; Texas is a right-to-work state.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor activity found at IAH in searches (AeroVect, TractEasy/EasyMile, Aurrigo, UISEE). Attractive target: United's largest hub, 1,100+ eGSE already deployed, new $177M GSE facility, and Terminal B capacity growth. Two AeroVect-relevant footholds: partner dnata runs IAH cargo, and partner GAT's new owner PrimeFlight is headquartered in nearby Sugar Land, TX. Main ramp requires a United (carrier) sale since United self-handles.

name: Hamad International Airport
owner: MATAR (Qatar Company for Airports Operation & Management)
aerovectNotes: Qatar Airways / QAS self-contained; capital-rich and autonomy-curious.

name: Ninoy Aquino International Airport

groundHandlers.name: MacroAsia Airport Services Corp (MASCORP)
groundHandlers.notes: Only ground handler present in NAIA Terminals 1, 2 and 3; 80% MacroAsia (Lucio Tan group, PAL-affiliated), 20% Konoike Transport of Japan. Source: https://macroasiacorp.com/macroasiaAirportServices

groundHandlers.name: 1Aviation Groundhandling Services Corp
groundHandlers.notes: Cebu Pacific took 60% control via debt-to-equity conversion Oct 2024; PAGSS/Jefferson Cheng hold 40%. Operates at 34 PH airports, 6,200+ staff; opened solar-powered ramp staging areas at NAIA T3/T4 Jan 2026. Sources: https://insiderph.com/cebu-pacific-retakes-control-of-ground-services-provider-after-6-years, https://www.linkedin.com/company/1aviation-groundhandling-services-corporation/

groundHandlers.name: PAGSS (Philippine Airport Ground Support Solutions)
groundHandlers.notes: Operating at NAIA since 1996 (as PAGS); also 40% owner of 1Aviation. Source: https://pagss.com/about-us

groundHandlers.name: dnata Philippines
groundHandlers.notes: Ground handling at NAIA plus Clark and Mactan-Cebu; also cargo handling for Qatar Airways Cargo and Air China Cargo. Notable because dnata is TractEasy's launch partner elsewhere (no MNL autonomy activity found). Source: https://www.dnata.com/en/global-network/philippines/

tailwinds.headline: San Miguel-led New NAIA Infra Corp (with Incheon Airport Corp) takes over NAIA, committing P170B to lift capacity from 35M to 62M passengers
tailwinds.relevance: A private operator with a 15-25 year concession and a large modernization budget creates a single commercial counterparty for airside innovation, replacing a slow government authority.

tailwinds.headline: Cebu Pacific deploys 13 TLD electric baggage tractors at NAIA Terminals 2 and 3 in partnership with NNIC
tailwinds.relevance: Active GSE fleet electrification shows carriers are already re-equipping the MNL ramp; new electric tractor fleets are natural retrofit candidates for AeroVect's driver-out kit.

tailwinds.headline: Cebu Pacific retakes 60% control of ground handler 1Aviation via debt-to-equity conversion to integrate ground ops and cut costs
tailwinds.relevance: A carrier consolidating its handler explicitly to reduce ground handling cost is a receptive buyer profile for autonomy-driven labor productivity gains.

operationsNotes: GSE model is mixed: carrier-affiliated handlers dominate (Cebu Pacific owns 60% of 1Aviation and deploys its own electric tractors; MacroAsia is tied to the Lucio Tan/PAL group), alongside independent third parties dnata Philippines and PAGSS. MASCORP is the only handler across Terminals 1-3. Airport is under a 15-year (extendable) San Miguel/Incheon concession (NNIC) since Sept 2024 with a P170B upgrade program including a new baggage handling system.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE trial or deployment found at MNL by TractEasy, Aurrigo, UISEE, or others; the ramp is open. Attractive: privatized operator (NNIC) spending heavily on modernization, carriers electrifying tractor fleets (retrofit-friendly), and dnata (TractEasy's partner at other airports) plus Cebu Pacific-owned 1Aviation as entry points. Watch-outs: NNIC's first-phase spend is on basic passenger infrastructure, and labor groups are already protesting the privatization, so job-displacement optics need careful handling.

name: Toronto Pearson International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Air Canada (self-handling)
groundHandlers.notes: Directly employs IAMAW-unionized Ramp Agents (Station Attendants) at Toronto Pearson per its own careers postings. Source: https://careers.aircanada.com/ca/en/job/AIPACICA38085EXTERNALENCA/Ramp-Agent-Station-Attendant-Airport-Full-Time

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Third-party ramp, passenger and cargo handler
groundHandlers.notes: Serves seven airlines at YYZ with WestJet as largest customer; ~530 IAM Local 2413 ground handling workers. Sources: https://iamaw.ca/iam-union-begins-bargaining-with-menzies-aviation-at-toronto-pearson/ and https://menziesaviation.com/our-network/toronto-yyz/

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.notes: Operates ground services and air cargo warehouses at YYZ; station page cites presence since 1958. Source: https://www.swissport.com/en/network/north-america/canada/yyz

groundHandlers.name: GTA dnata
groundHandlers.role: Third-party ground and cargo handler (dnata joint venture)
groundHandlers.notes: ISAGO-registered ground and cargo handler at YYZ serving a range of airline customers. Source: https://www.aviationpros.com/ground-support-worldwide/ground-handling/ground-handlers-service-providers/ramp-operations-training/press-release/21203359/dnata-gta-dnata-gains-isago-registration-in-toronto

tailwinds.headline: Toronto Pearson launches $3B first phase of LIFT modernization focused on airside and baggage infrastructure
tailwinds.relevance: Decade-long program targets 65M annual passengers; phase one includes ~30 km of new baggage systems, new aircraft stands, 2.2M sqm of airfield renewal and EV charging infrastructure, creating a rebuild window and electrification base favorable to retrofit autonomous GSE.

tailwinds.headline: Toronto Pearson launches DMZ and Creative Destruction Lab pilot programmes for AI and startup innovation
tailwinds.relevance: GTAA is explicitly recruiting startups and AI solutions for baggage handling, gate management and operational efficiency with a 20% productivity target, a direct procurement on-ramp for autonomous GSE vendors.

operationsNotes: Mixed GSE model: Air Canada, the dominant hub carrier, self-handles with its own IAMAW ramp agents, while WestJet and other carriers use third-party handlers (Menzies, Swissport, GTA dnata). Labor signals: Menzies baggage handler turnover at Pearson hit 160% in a year per CBC (https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/toronto/airport-workers-pearson-rally-1.4092577); Swissport ground crew ran a roughly 10-week strike at Pearson in 2017 (https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/toronto/pearson-strike-update-1.4348808); IAM opened new bargaining with Menzies at Pearson on Feb 26-27, 2026 covering ~530 workers (https://iamaw.ca/iam-union-begins-bargaining-with-menzies-aviation-at-toronto-pearson/); IAM also began Air Canada contract negotiations Feb 25, 2026 (https://iamaw.ca/iam-union-and-air-canada-begin-contract-negotiations/); Air Canada flight attendants struck in Aug 2025, disrupting Pearson operations (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Air_Canada_flight_attendants_strike).
aerovectNotes: No purpose-built autonomous baggage/cargo tractor vendor (TractEasy, Aurrigo, UISEE) found trialed or deployed at YYZ; only autonomy on the airfield is a 2023 Honda AWV inspection proof of concept, so the ramp is open. Attractive entry points: GTA dnata is a dnata joint venture and dnata is already an AeroVect trial/deployment partner globally; GTAA is actively soliciting startups for baggage handling and operational efficiency via DMZ and Creative Destruction Lab pilots (Oct 2025, 20% productivity target); the $3B LIFT phase one rebuilds airside and baggage infrastructure and adds EV charging, a natural window to introduce retrofit autonomy. Chronic ramp labor churn and multiple live union negotiations strengthen the labor-cost case.

competitors.status: proof-of-concept only: Honda prototype Autonomous Work Vehicle showcased Oct 2023 after a six-month collaboration; no production deployment reported

name: Chongqing Jiangbei International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Chongqing Jiangbei International Airport Ground Services Co., Ltd.
groundHandlers.role: Primary ramp and ground handler, subsidiary of state-owned Chongqing Airport Group; ~2,000 staff providing ramp, passenger and cargo services
groundHandlers.notes: Source: airport group official site https://www.cqa.cn/hkhy/35/

groundHandlers.name: Air China Chongqing Branch
groundHandlers.role: Carrier self-handling ground crew for its own flights
groundHandlers.notes: Actively recruiting ground crew for 2025; source: https://et.airchina.com.cn/cn/about_us/recruitment/ground_crew_info/184040.shtml

tailwinds.headline: CKG opens fourth runway with China's first BeiDou-integrated FOD detection system
tailwinds.relevance: Four-runway operation expands airside footprint and tow distances, increasing the case for autonomous baggage/cargo tractors; airport is already adopting airside sensing tech

tailwinds.headline: T3B satellite terminal opens, raising capacity to 80M passengers with remote apron tower and digital twin
tailwinds.relevance: Satellite-hall geometry means long inter-terminal baggage tows, the core autonomous-tractor use case; smart-apron infrastructure (remote tower, digital twin per caacsri.com) lowers barriers to autonomous ramp vehicles

tailwinds.headline: CKG deploys China-first robotic-arm baggage loading plus 13 autonomous mobile robots for baggage transport at T3A
tailwinds.relevance: Demonstrates active investment in baggage-handling automation and robotics; adjacent step to apron autonomous tractors, though currently inside the terminal only

operationsNotes: CKG became China's third four-runway airport (after Pudong and Daxing) in Dec 2024; T3B satellite terminal (world's largest standalone satellite hall) opened April 2025, lifting capacity to 80M passengers, 1.2M tonnes cargo, 580K movements. T3B uses a remote apron tower replacing line-of-sight apron control plus a nationwide-first digital twin system built by CAAC Second Research Institute (https://www.caacsri.com/development/detail/1570.html). Annual passengers first exceeded 50M in 2025. Labor signal is national, not airport-specific: China civil aviation talent gap of about 478,000 (skewed to pilots/maintenance) per Sina Finance Jan 2025 (https://finance.sina.com.cn/roll/2025-01-11/doc-ineerpnp4632719.shtml), with active ground-crew recruiting at CKG by Air China.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor found on the CKG ramp; automation to date is terminal-side (robotic-arm baggage loading, 13 baggage AMRs at T3A) and tower-side (remote apron tower, digital twin at T3B). Airport shows strong automation appetite and huge scale (4 runways, 80M pax capacity, 50M+ pax in 2025 per https://m.12371.gov.cn/content/2025-12/31/content_504911.html), but the ramp is run by a state-owned airport-group subsidiary and Chinese airports source autonomy from domestic vendors (UISEE already deployed at Urumqi and Pudong per https://www.uisee.com/en/about.html and https://www.metal.com/en/newscontent/103437668), so access for a US retrofit vendor is constrained. Watch, not prospect.

name: Chengdu Shuangliu International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Sichuan Province Airport Group Aviation Ground Services Co.
groundHandlers.notes: Founded 2003, wholly owned by Sichuan Province Airport Group (the airport operator). At Shuangliu it handles 6 domestic airlines (China Eastern, Chengdu Airlines, Loong Air, SF Airlines, China Postal Airlines, NW Cargo) plus 9 international/regional all-cargo carriers, per Sichuan Modern Logistics Association profile (scxd56.net) and Baidu Baike listing of group subsidiaries.

groundHandlers.name: Sichuan Airlines Logistics Co. (SAL)
groundHandlers.notes: Wholly owned Sichuan Airlines subsidiary headquartered at Shuangliu, 800+ employees, 600+ tonnes/day (routesonline.com). Its CTU cargo terminal hosts the Westwell Q-Tractor autonomous deployment.

groundHandlers.name: Air China (Southwest hub)
groundHandlers.role: Hub carrier with own base operations
groundHandlers.notes: CTU is an Air China hub per Air China's Chengdu airport guide; Sichuan Airlines, Chengdu Airlines and several other carriers are also based at CTU (Wikipedia).

competitors.status: pilot with Sichuan Airlines Logistics launched March 2025 at the Shuangliu cargo terminal; completed full-process autonomous transport verification within the cargo station

tailwinds.headline: Shuangliu reopens renovated Terminal 1, restoring dual-terminal operations as Chengdu's dual-airport system nears its 105M design capacity within 3-4 years
tailwinds.relevance: Sustained throughput growth and capacity pressure at China's third-largest aviation hub raise ramp-equipment utilization and the payoff from GSE automation.

tailwinds.headline: CTU cargo throughput up 17.9% YoY to 540,000 tons in first three quarters of 2025 amid aviation-economy zone build-out
tailwinds.relevance: Fast cargo growth is the exact use case for autonomous cargo/baggage tractors; also 24.9M pax (+2.4%) in the same period.

tailwinds.headline: Westwell and Sichuan Airlines Logistics complete autonomous tractor pilot at CTU cargo terminal and pass acceptance
tailwinds.relevance: Proves regulatory and operational acceptance of L4 tow tractors on live CTU cargo routes, but the win is held by a domestic competitor rather than being open whitespace.

operationsNotes: CTU became primarily domestic after Tianfu (TFU) opened; renovated T1 (reopened Feb 24, 2025) serves cross-strait flights, limited international transit, and all Sichuan Airlines domestic routes. GSE model is mixed: the airport group's wholly owned ground services company handles many domestic and all-cargo carriers, while hub carriers (Sichuan Airlines via SAL for cargo, Air China) run their own base operations. Confirmed autonomy is at the cargo terminal; no evidence found of autonomous baggage tractors on the passenger ramp.
aerovectNotes: Westwell's Q-Tractor is deployed at the Sichuan Airlines (SAL) cargo terminal on this airport after a March 2025 pilot passed acceptance. Broader China context is hostile for a US retrofit vendor: UISEE reports a 90.5% share of the Greater China airport L4 market with 17 mainland airport deployments (per its HK IPO filing coverage). Treat CTU as a competitor reference site, not a prospect.

name: Kunming Changshui International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Yunnan Airport Group (Kunming Airport)
groundHandlers.role: Airport operator; state-owned group that runs KMG and coordinates ramp/passenger handling with its affiliated ground service company
groundHandlers.notes: Official airport site confirms Yunnan Airport Group operates KMG; a Feb 2025 report describes the airport working with its ground service company (地服公司) on passenger/flight support. Sources: https://km.ynairport.com/general.jhtml and https://finance.sina.com.cn/jjxw/2025-02-26/doc-inemvnmc8687307.shtml

groundHandlers.name: Yunnan Airport Logistics Co (云南空港物流)
groundHandlers.role: Cargo, mail and baggage ground transport support at Kunming airport
groundHandlers.notes: Yunnan Airport Group controlled subsidiary, founded 2002, NEEQ-listed 2019; core business is airport cargo/mail/baggage ground handling support. Source: https://yn.yunnan.cn/system/2019/04/16/030254161.shtml

groundHandlers.name: China Eastern Yunnan (东航云南)
groundHandlers.role: Largest base carrier at KMG (one of 6 base airlines); self-handling status on the ramp unverified
groundHandlers.notes: Kunming-based China Eastern subsidiary certified 2011, ~74 aircraft as of Jan 2026. Source: https://zh.wikipedia.org/zh-cn/%E4%B8%9C%E8%88%AA%E4%BA%91%E5%8D%97%E5%85%AC%E5%8F%B8

tailwinds.headline: AIIB-financed Kunming Changshui Airport Expansion and Green Development Project includes electrified airfield service vehicles and on-airport solar/storage; first CNY 550M disbursement expected Q4 2025
tailwinds.relevance: Funded GSE electrification and green-airport program means the KMG tractor fleet is being renewed, the natural window for autonomy-ready equipment

tailwinds.headline: North section of KMG's new east second runway passed civil aviation industry acceptance on Feb 6, 2026, moving toward operation
tailwinds.relevance: Expansion adds runway and apron capacity ahead of the 2028 T2 opening, increasing ramp tow distances and GSE demand

operationsNotes: KMG is a 4F airport and one of China's eight regional hubs, operated by state-owned Yunnan Airport Group, with two independent runways of 4,000m and 4,500m (https://km.ynairport.com/general.jhtml). A CNY 64.07B expansion (T2 terminal 730,000 sqm with 164 stands, West 2 and East 2 runways, new cargo terminal) broke ground Nov 2022 with planned use around Dec 2028 (https://www.seetao.com/details/192456.html). Six base airlines operate at KMG, led by China Eastern Yunnan (https://zh.wikipedia.org/zh-cn/%E4%B8%9C%E8%88%AA%E4%BA%91%E5%8D%97%E5%85%AC%E5%8F%B8).
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE trial or deployment found at KMG in English or Chinese searches. Context: CAAC published a national Airport Driverless Equipment Application Roadmap 2021-2025 (http://www.caac.gov.cn/XXGK/XXGK/ZCJD/202112/t20211208_210451.html) and domestic vendor UISEE claims deployments at ~20 Chinese airports incl. Hong Kong and a 40-tractor driverless baggage fleet at Urumqi (https://forkliftyp.com/dynamics/5166.html), so any KMG autonomy program would very likely go to a domestic vendor. State-owned operator plus China market access make this constrained for a US retrofit vendor despite a large expansion and AIIB-funded GSE electrification; watch, not prospect.

name: Shanghai Hongqiao International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Shanghai International Airport Services Co. (SIAS)
groundHandlers.role: Third-party ground handler at both Hongqiao and Pudong
groundHandlers.notes: JV formed 2012: Shanghai International Airport Co 41%, Shanghai Airport Authority 10%, Air China 24%, Cathay Pacific's HAS 25%. Absorbed the airport authority's existing ground handling staff. Source: https://news.cathaypacific.com/shanghai-airport-authority-shanghai-international-airport-co-ltd-air-china-and-cathay-pacific-establish-airport-ground-handling-joint-venture-in-shanghai

groundHandlers.name: China Eastern Airlines (hub carrier)
groundHandlers.role: Dominant hub carrier headquartered on the Hongqiao airport grounds; self-handles via group units
groundHandlers.notes: Its JV Shanghai Eastern Aircraft Maintenance Ltd (SEAM, with China Aircraft Services Ltd) provides line maintenance and aircraft ground handling at both Hongqiao and Pudong. Sources: https://www.casl.com.hk/en-us/associate-companies and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China_Eastern_Airlines

groundHandlers.name: Shanghai Airlines / Juneyao Air (based carriers)
groundHandlers.notes: Hongqiao is a hub for China Eastern, Shanghai Airlines and Juneyao Air per https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghai_Hongqiao_International_Airport

tailwinds.headline: UISEE T30A autonomous baggage tractor enters trial operations at Shanghai Pudong under the same Shanghai Airport Authority group
tailwinds.relevance: The airport group that runs SHA is already piloting L4 autonomous baggage tractors at its sister hub, integrated with the TAM system. Validates the use case for Hongqiao but also entrenches a domestic vendor.

tailwinds.headline: CAAC approves smart-airport pilot at Shanghai Hongqiao with China Eastern and East China ATMB: One ID biometrics, RFID full-journey baggage tracking, runway status lights, airport-ATC data integration
tailwinds.relevance: Regulator-sanctioned automation and data-sharing program at SHA itself; the RFID baggage tracking and airport-airline data integration are the kind of digital infrastructure autonomous GSE dispatch depends on.

tailwinds.headline: Hongqiao annual passenger volume exceeded 50 million for the first time in 2025; express shuttle routes are 72% of operations
tailwinds.relevance: Record throughput at a slot-constrained two-runway airport with high-frequency shuttle banks raises pressure on ramp turnaround efficiency, the core autonomous-tug value proposition.

operationsNotes: SHA handled 50,151,025 passengers in 2025, 8th busiest in China (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghai_Hongqiao_International_Airport). Primarily domestic hub for China Eastern, Shanghai Airlines and Juneyao Air with limited international service; express flights 72% of operations (https://www.chinadaily.com.cn/a/202603/23/WS69c0f111a310d6866eb3f5e2.html). Ground handling is mixed: airport-affiliated JV SIAS serves both Shanghai airports alongside hub-carrier self-handling by the China Eastern group. Labor pressure is country-level: China's civil aviation lost over 10% of certificated staff 2019-2021 with ground service departures and rehiring difficulty (https://finance.sina.cn/chanjing/gsxw/2022-12-19/detail-imxxfkrw0641217.d.html); Chinese airports were still reworking talent strategies against frontline shortages in late 2025 (https://ttgchina.com/2025/12/12/%E6%9C%BA%E5%9C%BA%E9%87%8D%E5%A1%91%E4%BA%BA%E6%89%8D%E6%88%98%E7%95%A5-%E5%BA%94%E5%AF%B9%E5%BD%93%E4%BB%A3%E6%8C%91%E6%88%98/). No SHA-specific strike or shortage reports found.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE activity found on the SHA ramp itself, so not competitor_held. But constrained as a prospect: domestic vendor UISEE is already trialing autonomous baggage tractors at sister airport PVG under the same Shanghai Airport Authority group (and at HKIA/Guangzhou), and China's regulatory and procurement environment strongly favors domestic autonomy vendors over a US retrofit provider. Watch for UISEE expansion to Hongqiao, which would flip this to competitor_held.

competitors.status: Trial at sister airport Shanghai Pudong (PVG), not on the SHA ramp: T30A L4 autonomous baggage tractor began trial ops July 2025 with Feeyo Technology, positioned to become China's first AV project integrated with the Total Airport Management (TAM) system. Same Shanghai Airport Authority group operates SHA, so expansion to Hongqiao is a plausible next step.

name: Xi'an Xianyang International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Western Airport Group Aviation Ground Handling (Xi'an) Co. (西部机场集团航空地勤(西安)有限公司)
groundHandlers.role: Primary third-party handler; wholly owned subsidiary of China West Airport Group (CWAG)
groundHandlers.notes: Provides ground agency services for domestic and international scheduled flights, charters, business jets and freighters at XIY per its official company profile (hkdq.cwag.com). CWAG is a Shaanxi provincial SOE operating 18 airports.

groundHandlers.name: Western Airport Group Aviation Logistics Co. (西部机场集团航空物流有限公司)
groundHandlers.role: Cargo terminal and airside cargo logistics operator
groundHandlers.notes: CWAG subsidiary running the cargo terminal; it is the operating partner for the UISEE driverless tractor deployment (uisee.com case page).

groundHandlers.name: China Eastern Airlines (Xi'an hub)
groundHandlers.role: Hub carrier with own ground service units
groundHandlers.notes: CEA's XIY airport page describes its own loading/unloading department, special vehicle fleet and line maintenance serving its and agented carriers' flights (ceair.com); T5 opened with a CEA flight. Supports a mixed handler-led/carrier-led model.

competitors.status: deployed
deployed since early 2023; cargo tractors between domestic cargo terminal and T3 apron staging area with CWAG Aviation Logistics

tailwinds.headline: Terminal 5 opens as core of phase-3 expansion; airport designed for 83M passengers and 1M tonnes cargo annually
tailwinds.relevance: Major capacity step-up with automated baggage handling (7-minute average) in a CAAC 'benchmark' smart-airport project; more ramp traffic means more tug movements and a stated appetite for airside automation.

tailwinds.headline: CAAC News: robotic arms and intelligent vehicles adopted for baggage transport at Xi'an Xianyang airport
tailwinds.relevance: Shows XIY actively pushing baggage-handling automation onto the ramp in 2025, a direct adjacency to autonomous baggage tractors; also signals incumbent vendors embedding deeper.

tailwinds.headline: Calibration flights completed on XIY's new second south runway
tailwinds.relevance: Phase-3 expansion continues beyond T5: added runway and apron scale increases towing distances and GSE demand across the airfield.

operationsNotes: XIY is CWAG's hub airport. T5 (700,000 sqm, 50M pax design) opened 2025-02-20 as part of the phase-3 expansion toward 83M passengers and 1M tonnes cargo per year, with automated baggage handling and other smart-airport systems; a new south runway completed calibration flights in July 2025. Ground handling is dominated by CWAG subsidiaries with hub carrier China Eastern running its own ground service units, i.e. a mixed model.
aerovectNotes: UISEE driverless cargo tractors have operated on XIY's ramp with CWAG's own logistics subsidiary since early 2023 (vendor case study plus trade press; March 2023 milestone of one AV replacing one driver), and Leador Spatial ran an unmanned logistics trial in July 2023. Buyer is a state-owned airport group procuring domestic autonomy vendors directly under CAAC's driverless-equipment roadmap, so market access for a US retrofit vendor is effectively closed. Track as competitor reference site rather than a prospect.

name: London Gatwick Airport

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.notes: Swissport lists LGW as a station; UK's largest GHA handling ~25% of UK flights. Source: https://www.swissport.com/en/network/europe/united-kingdom/lgw

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.notes: One of the UK's two largest third-party handlers, active at Gatwick alongside Swissport and dnata. Source: https://www.aviationpros.com/ground-support-worldwide/ground-handling/ground-handlers-service-providers/news/11612041/menzies-could-replace-swissport-as-no-1-baggage-handler-at-gatwick

groundHandlers.name: dnata
groundHandlers.role: Third-party ground, cargo and transport handler
groundHandlers.notes: UK operations based at London Gatwick; ~17,500 turnarounds annually across UK for 40+ airlines. Source: https://www.dnata.com/en/global-network/united-kingdom/

groundHandlers.name: DHL Supply Chain
groundHandlers.role: Contracted handler for easyJet (Gatwick's largest carrier)
groundHandlers.notes: Took over easyJet Gatwick handling Nov 2017; contract extended to 2027 covering bag drop, sorting, loading/unloading. Source: https://www.dhl.com/gb-en/home/press/press-archive/2022/dhl-extends-contract-with-easyjet-to-manage-ground-handling-operations-at-london-gatwick.html

groundHandlers.name: Gatwick Ground Services (GGS)
groundHandlers.role: British Airways wholly owned self-handling subsidiary
groundHandlers.notes: Aurrigo's Apr 2026 announcement describes GGS as a wholly owned BA subsidiary that grew from 340 to 1,200+ staff at Gatwick. Source: https://aurrigo.com/news/

groundHandlers.name: Red Handling
groundHandlers.notes: 100+ baggage handlers, check-in staff and dispatchers struck in May 2025 over missed pension payments and late wages. Source: https://www.unitetheunion.org/news-events/news/2025/may/red-handling-strike-at-gatwick-suspended-as-unite-members-are-balloted

competitors.status: deployed: live airside operation with British Airways/IAG; entered full-time service at Gatwick after Heathrow tests, starting with one vehicle with scope to scale; Aurrigo's own careers page names Gatwick as the primary customer site for its airside safety-operator field team, indicating ongoing live operations as of 2026
programme origin: Oct 2023 IAG deal covering Auto-DollyTug, Auto-Cargo and Auto-Sim, with a 250K GBP initial trial at a large UK airport and deployment targeted for H1 2025

tailwinds.headline: Gatwick Northern Runway expansion approved, taking capacity to ~80M passengers with a 2.2B GBP privately financed programme
tailwinds.relevance: Dual-runway operations mean materially more turnarounds and GSE movements on a constrained ramp, raising the value of tug automation

tailwinds.headline: UK High Court dismisses judicial review challenges, keeping the Northern Runway development consent in force
tailwinds.relevance: Expansion is now legally de-risked, so handlers and carriers can commit to fleet and automation investment for the capacity ramp

tailwinds.headline: Aurrigo hires ex-Gatwick Ground Services (BA subsidiary) MD Mark Gower as Director of Global Airport Operations
tailwinds.relevance: Shows autonomy adoption is accelerating at Gatwick; also a competitive signal that Aurrigo is entrenching with the BA/GGS relationship

operationsNotes: London's second airport, dominated by easyJet (handled by DHL Supply Chain under contract to 2027) with BA self-handling via wholly owned GGS; third-party handlers Swissport, Menzies and dnata also operate on the ramp, and Norwegian uses Red Handling. GSE model is mixed: carrier-owned self-handling (GGS, Red Handling) alongside independent handlers. Ramp labor is visibly stressed: Red Handling baggage handlers struck four Sundays in May 2025 over pension and pay failures, and ICTS baggage screeners struck over pay in Aug 2025 (after a postponed July 2024 action), winning a 7% raise.
aerovectNotes: Aurrigo Auto-DollyTug is live on the LGW ramp with BA/IAG and Gatwick is Aurrigo's primary field-team site, so the airport is competitor-held on the BA/GGS side. However, Aurrigo sells purpose-built new vehicles tied to the IAG relationship; the third-party handlers (Swissport, Menzies, dnata, DHL for easyJet) run large conventional tug fleets and face acute labor pressure, which suits AeroVect's retrofit RaaS model. A wedge via easyJet/DHL (largest operation at LGW) or Menzies/dnata is plausible, but expect an autonomy-aware airport authority and an incumbent vendor reference on site.

name: Boston Logan International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.role: Ramp handling, passenger services, baggage, fueling, cargo
groundHandlers.notes: At BOS since 1978; facilities at Terminal E and a cargo warehouse on Harborside Drive. Source: https://www.swissport.com/en/network/north-america/united-states/bos

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.notes: Confirmed BOS station page. Source: https://menziesaviation.com/our-network/boston-logan-bos/

groundHandlers.name: Unifi
groundHandlers.notes: Active job listings at BOS Terminal E; Delta holds ~49% minority stake in Unifi nationally. Source: https://www.indeed.com/q-unifi-l-boston-logan-international-airport,-ma-jobs.html

groundHandlers.name: PrimeFlight (incl. former GAT)
groundHandlers.notes: PrimeFlight location at 18 Logan Way, Boston (https://www.yelp.com/biz/primeflight-aviation-services-boston); acquired AeroVect partner GAT on Feb 5, 2026 (https://www.primeflight.com/node/572)

groundHandlers.name: dnata
groundHandlers.notes: Catering facility supplying up to 12,500 meals/day; no BOS ramp handling found. Source: https://www.dnata.com/media-centre/dnata-adds-boston-and-los-angeles-to-its-global-catering-network/

groundHandlers.name: JetBlue (self-handling carrier)
groundHandlers.notes: JetBlue, the largest BOS carrier, hires its own ramp crews at Boston. Source: https://careers.jetblue.com/job/Boston-Ground-Operations-Crew-MA-02128/1354576300/

tailwinds.headline: Massport releases 10-year strategic plan for Logan: terminal core modernization, ATC tower renovation, remote terminals
tailwinds.relevance: A decade-long modernization program signals openness to new airside technology and creates procurement windows for automation on the ramp.

tailwinds.headline: Massport adopts $4.1B FY2026-2030 capital plan including $315M toward net-zero by 2031
tailwinds.relevance: Large capital cycle plus dedicated decarbonization budget; plan also targets ~$175M in operating expense cuts over five years, which favors labor-saving autonomous GSE.

tailwinds.headline: Massport reports 374 airside charging ports for tenant-owned GSE as tenants electrify tugs and tractors at Logan
tailwinds.relevance: Existing airside charging infrastructure and an electrifying tenant GSE fleet lower the barrier to deploying retrofit autonomous electric baggage tractors. Companion page: https://www.massport.com/environment/roadmap-to-net-zero/ev-fleet-transition

operationsNotes: Mixed GSE model: JetBlue (largest BOS carrier) self-handles with its own Ground Operations Crew, while Swissport, Menzies, Unifi, and PrimeFlight provide third-party handling, concentrated around international operations at Terminal E and cargo. Labor: a ten-union Coalition of Logan Airport Workers (incl. 32BJ SEIU) rallied May 1, 2026 to overturn Massport's 10-person demonstration cap, citing unaffordable health coverage and immigration-related workforce losses (https://www.seiu32bj.org/press-release/collation-representing-logan-airport-workers-rallies-on-may-day-to-demand-the-right-of-assembly/); prior contracted-worker strikes at Logan documented by 32BJ. No active ramp strike found as of Jul 2026.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous GSE vendor found on the BOS ramp. Channel angles: PrimeFlight (acquirer of AeroVect partner GAT, Feb 2026) has a Boston location though its BOS service scope is unconfirmed; dnata (AeroVect customer for ~100 retrofit units, per https://theloadstar.com/dnata-solves-airport-labour-shortage-with-aerovect-autonomous-vehicle-orders/) is catering-only at BOS. Risk vector: Swissport, Aurrigo's launch partner at ZRH, runs ramp and cargo at BOS. Massport's capital cycle, net-zero 2031 program, and airside eGSE charging build-out make BOS attractive for an electric retrofit pitch.

name: Rome–Fiumicino Leonardo da Vinci International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Airport Handling S.p.A. (dnata majority-owned)
groundHandlers.notes: Won 7-year ADR license, launched Feb 2025, absorbed ~1,800 ex-Swissport workers, ~50% market share at FCO. Committed 20M+ euro GSE investment, over 40% electric fleet. Sources: https://www.dnata.com/media-centre/airport-handling-cleared-to-launch-operations-at-rome-fiumicino-airport-fco/ and https://www.editorialedomani.it/fatti/airport-handling-swissport-aeroporto-fiumicino-cosa-accade-appalto-ex-dipendenti-alitalia-ita-uvbau5gu

groundHandlers.name: Aviapartner Italia
groundHandlers.notes: One of three winners of the Oct 2023 ADR ramp handling tender; operates at FCO. Sources: https://www.aviapartner.aero/rome-fiumicino and https://www.romatoday.it/economia/swissport-esclusa-aeroporto-fiumicino.html

groundHandlers.name: Aviation Services
groundHandlers.notes: Third winner of the ADR ramp handling tender alongside Airport Handling and Aviapartner. Source: https://www.romatoday.it/economia/swissport-esclusa-aeroporto-fiumicino.html

groundHandlers.name: Alha Group
groundHandlers.notes: Cargo city warehouses at FCO since 2009; physical and document handling, CEIV Pharma cool center. Source: https://alhagroup.com/en/terminal/fiumicino-fco/

groundHandlers.name: Swissport (exited)
groundHandlers.notes: Handled ~50% of FCO traffic from July 2022; lost the ADR tender, appealed to the Lazio court, and ceased FCO operations Feb 2025. Sources: https://www.swissport.com/en/news/current-news/2024/tender-decision-announced-by-aeroporti-di-roma-for-rome-fiumicino and https://www.editorialedomani.it/fatti/airport-handling-swissport-aeroporto-fiumicino-cosa-accade-appalto-ex-dipendenti-alitalia-ita-uvbau5gu

tailwinds.headline: dnata's Airport Handling cleared to launch at FCO with 20M+ euro GSE investment, 40%+ electric fleet
tailwinds.relevance: A new handler standing up a fresh GSE fleet (large electric share) means procurement is active and fleet decisions are recent; retrofit autonomy could ride the same modernization budget.

tailwinds.headline: Aeroporti di Roma deploys Outsight 3D LiDAR Physical AI at scale across Fiumicino
tailwinds.relevance: Terminal-side (not ramp), but proves ADR scales AI/LiDAR pilots to airport-wide deployments through its Runway to the Future innovation program, a ready pathway for an autonomous-GSE pilot.

tailwinds.headline: ADR launches WHILL autonomous mobility chairs at FCO
tailwinds.relevance: ADR already operates autonomous vehicles in live passenger environments, signaling regulatory and operational comfort with autonomy at FCO.

operationsNotes: GSE is handler-led: ADR's Oct 2023 tender limited FCO ramp handling to three licensed providers (Airport Handling, Aviapartner Italia, Aviation Services), each owning/investing in its own GSE. Swissport exited Feb 19, 2025 after losing the tender and court appeals (Lazio court upheld the award). Minor exception: Aeroitalia began self-handling parts of its own chain (e.g., apron buses) at FCO in 2026 (https://www.theflightclub.it/en/2026/03/aeroitalia-handling-autoproduction-fiumicino/amp/). Labor: repeated nationwide ground-handling strikes hit FCO, including Sept 26, 2025 (https://www.euronews.com/travel/2025/09/25/flights-in-italy-face-disruption-as-nationwide-air-transport-strikes-hit-on-friday), Oct 13 and 29, 2025 walkouts (https://adept.travel/news/2025-10-09-italy-airport-worker-walkouts-oct-13-29), and a 24-hour ground-handling strike June 26, 2026 with FCO fully affected (https://www.traveltourister.com/news/italy-june-26-2026-ground-handling-strike-rome-milan-venice-naples-eu261-rights/); the 2025 handler transition also left ~200 temporary workers unabsorbed (https://www.editorialedomani.it/fatti/airport-handling-swissport-aeroporto-fiumicino-cosa-accade-appalto-ex-dipendenti-alitalia-ita-uvbau5gu).
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor found on the FCO ramp. Attractive entry conditions: ramp market fully reshuffled in Feb 2025 with three licensed handlers, active GSE capex (Airport Handling 20M+ euro, 40%+ electric), high strike-driven labor pressure in Italy, and an airport operator (ADR) that runs an innovation program already scaling LiDAR/autonomy deployments. Caveat: dnata group (Airport Handling parent) is an EZTow/TractEasy user elsewhere per briefing context, so dnata is both the largest door in and a potential channel for TractEasy; Aviapartner and Aviation Services are uncommitted alternatives. Ramp access requires working within ADR's three-handler license regime.

name: Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Hangzhou Xiaoshan International Airport Co. Ltd.
groundHandlers.role: Airport operator and primary ground handler
groundHandlers.notes: Listed as both airport authority and handling agent (Business Air News handbook). Typical Chinese model: airport company's ground services arm runs the ramp and owns GSE, so handler_led with the handler being the airport itself.

groundHandlers.name: Mainland GroundExpress
groundHandlers.notes: Listed as agent at HGH (Business Air News handbook).

groundHandlers.name: Deer Jet
groundHandlers.notes: FBO at HGH per Business Air News handbook; not a commercial ramp handler.

competitors.status: same trial reported by Hangzhou municipal government: East China's first apron driverless vehicles, 8-ton loads, 4 km routes, claimed 90% risk reduction, 20% efficiency gain, 15% cost reduction, full-scale deployment expected early 2026

tailwinds.headline: Hangzhou airport launches East China's first driverless apron vehicles, full-scale deployment planned early 2026
tailwinds.relevance: Airport-endorsed autonomy program under CAAC's unmanned airport equipment roadmap proves regulatory and operational acceptance of autonomous tractors on this ramp, but the slot is being filled by domestic vendor UISEE.

tailwinds.headline: Cross-border e-commerce lifts air cargo at Hangzhou airport: exports +19.6% H1 2025, new Tokyo/Tashkent/Ostrava cargo routes
tailwinds.relevance: Fast-growing e-commerce cargo volumes increase demand for tug capacity and repetitive cargo-dolly runs, the core autonomous-GSE use case at this airport.

tailwinds.headline: HGH passes 50M passengers in 2025 (+5.0%), ranking 7th in China
tailwinds.relevance: Traffic growth against a fixed ramp workforce raises throughput pressure that automation programs are explicitly meant to relieve.

operationsNotes: 7th busiest airport in China: 50.46M passengers (+5.0%), 792,710 t cargo (-7.9%), 329,355 movements in 2025 (Wikipedia). T4 (720,000 sqm, 50M pax design capacity) opened Sep 2022 with a third runway under Phase III. Major cross-border e-commerce cargo hub: H1 2025 international exports 97,500 t (+19.6%), e-commerce over 60% of export volume, 20 active international cargo routes. Notable airside feature: a 700 m northeast airside tunnel with weak GPS, which UISEE addressed with semantic localization, indicating nontrivial autonomy operating environment.
aerovectNotes: Effectively closed near-term. Domestic competitor UISEE has been trialing autonomous cargo tractors on the T4 apron since Dec 2024 with airport-stated full-scale deployment targeted early 2026; if normalized ops are confirmed this flips to competitor_held. Ramp is run by the state-owned airport company itself, and Chinese airport procurement under the CAAC unmanned-equipment roadmap strongly favors domestic AV vendors, a structural barrier for a US retrofit vendor. Value mainly as competitive intelligence on UISEE's China playbook (also expanding with Xinjiang Airport Group per May 2026 coverage) rather than as a sales target.

name: Tan Son Nhat International Airport

groundHandlers.name: SAGS (Saigon Ground Services)
groundHandlers.role: Independent third-party ramp/ground handler, subsidiary of Airports Corporation of Vietnam (ACV)
groundHandlers.notes: Leading handler at SGN for international and domestic carriers. Lost Vietjet (nearly 40% of 2024 revenue) in April 2025; now investing ~$2M in staff and equipment for Long Thanh ramp-up. Sources: https://sags.vn/ramp-services.html, https://www.marketscreener.com/quote/stock/SAIGON-GROUND-SERVICES-46535955/news/Saigon-Ground-Services-Joint-Stock-Company-Announces-Termination-of-Service-Contract-with-VietJet-Av-49674134/

groundHandlers.name: VIAGS (Vietnam Airport Ground Services)
groundHandlers.notes: Formed Jan 2016 by merging Vietnam Airlines ground-service units at Tan Son Nhat, Noi Bai, and Da Nang; handles Vietnam Airlines group and third parties at SGN. Source: https://www.viags.vn/en-US/about-viags

groundHandlers.name: Vietjet (self-handling)
groundHandlers.role: Carrier self-handling its own ramp since April 20, 2025
groundHandlers.notes: Terminated SAGS contract and stood up 1,000+ own ground staff at SGN; transition caused widespread delays April 20-21, 2025 and a CAAV directive to fix staffing. Sources: https://en.sggp.org.vn/vietjet-hit-by-flight-delays-amid-holiday-rush-ground-service-transition-post117181.html

tailwinds.headline: Tan Son Nhat Terminal 3 opens, lifting airport capacity to 50M passengers a year
tailwinds.relevance: 20M pax/yr of new domestic capacity means more turns and more baggage-tractor movements on a constrained ramp, strengthening the throughput case for autonomous tugs.

tailwinds.headline: Vietjet ends SAGS contract and takes ground handling in-house at SGN from April 20, 2025
tailwinds.relevance: A major carrier is building a ramp operation and GSE fleet from scratch while short on experienced staff, a natural opening for a retrofit autonomy vendor; also pressures SAGS to differentiate on cost and technology.

tailwinds.headline: SAGS secures United Airlines deal and commits ~VND53B ($2M) for staff, vehicles, and systems at Long Thanh ahead of late-2026 opening
tailwinds.relevance: Active GSE and systems procurement cycle across the HCMC airport system; retrofittable tractors bought now for SGN/Long Thanh could carry AeroVect kits.

operationsNotes: Terminal 3 (domestic) opened April 2025, adding 20M pax/yr capacity for a 50M total (https://www.tradelinkmedia.biz/publications/7/news/5823). Vietnam targets moving long-haul/international traffic to Long Thanh from late 2026, ~90% of international by 2027 (https://aviationweek.com/air-transport/airports-networks/vietnam-targets-long-haul-shift-long-thanh-2026). Labor signals: Vietnam-wide aviation human-resource shortage across ground operations and skilled roles (https://hr.asia/top-news/vietnam/the-vietnamese-aviation-industry-faces-human-resource-shortage/); CAAV publicly ordered Vietjet to address ground staff shortages after mass delays during its April 2025 self-handling switch at SGN (https://vietnamnet.vn/en/vietnam-s-aviation-authority-demands-vietjet-address-staff-shortages-2394164.html). No strike activity found.
aerovectNotes: No autonomy vendor found on the SGN ramp, and three distinct GSE operators (SAGS, VIAGS, Vietjet in-house) plus documented aviation labor shortages make it interesting. Constrained because ACV plans to shift ~90% of international traffic to Long Thanh by 2027, SGN becomes mostly domestic, and Vietnam airside autonomy regulation is unproven. Best angle may be the whole HCMC system: handlers are procuring new fleets for Long Thanh now, so a retrofit pitch could ride that equipment cycle.

name: Sydney Kingsford Smith International Airport

groundHandlers.name: dnata
groundHandlers.role: Ramp, baggage and cargo handler for 20+ international carriers incl. Emirates, Singapore Airlines, Cathay Pacific, Delta, China Eastern/Southern (per SYD baggage handler directory)
groundHandlers.notes: Workers struck for 24 hours in Dec 2024 across NSW and other states over pay and guaranteed hours

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Ramp/baggage handler for Air Canada, ANA, BA, Qatar, Korean Air, United and others; also runs three Sydney cargo facilities (~250,000 tpa capacity)
groundHandlers.notes: Opened third SYD cargo warehouse M1 in Sept 2025; won SriLankan Airlines SYD ground handling from May 2026

groundHandlers.name: Swissport
groundHandlers.role: Ramp/baggage handler for American, Air India, Fiji Airways, Hawaiian, AirAsia X and others; also performs outsourced Qantas ground work nationally per TWU
groundHandlers.notes: Qantas outsourced its in-house ground handling in 2020, later ruled unlawful; work went to third-party handlers

groundHandlers.name: Star Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Handles Jetstar Airways at SYD per airport baggage handler directory

tailwinds.headline: Sydney Airport releases preliminary draft Master Plan 2045: 72M annual passengers, T2-T3 super terminal, airfreight doubling to 1.4M tonnes
tailwinds.relevance: 75% passenger growth and doubled cargo on a constrained site raises ramp throughput pressure, strengthening the case for autonomous tugs that add capacity without headcount

tailwinds.headline: Menzies Aviation opens third Sydney cargo facility (M1), lifting SYD cargo footprint to 20,000+ sqm and up to 250,000 tonnes/yr
tailwinds.relevance: Growing multi-facility cargo operation with airside access creates repetitive terminal-to-aircraft tows, a core AeroVect retrofit use case with an existing global handler account

tailwinds.headline: Menzies Aviation wins SriLankan Airlines ground handling contract at Sydney Airport (3 years from May 2026, ~AUD 8.3M)
tailwinds.relevance: Active contract churn among SYD handlers shows competition on cost and service, a wedge for autonomy-enabled differentiation in bids

operationsNotes: Qantas no longer self-handles: its 2020 outsourcing of ~1,820 ground handling jobs was ruled unlawful, ending in a $90M penalty (Aug 2025) on top of $120M compensation; work sits with third-party handlers incl. Swissport. dnata ramp/cargo/baggage workers took 24-hour strike action in Dec 2024 across NSW and four other states after 98% voted for action, seeking better pay and guaranteed hours (many guaranteed only 24 hrs/week). TWU continued pressing pay disparity issues in Aug 2025 (Perth dnata workers told to move to Sydney for higher pay). Sydney Airport commissioned an eGSE/fixed ground services study (Beca) showing up to 69% ground-servicing emissions reduction potential, but no dated airport-wide GSE electrification commitment was found.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE trial or deployment found at SYD from TractEasy/EasyMile, Aurrigo, UISEE or others. Ramp is handler_led (dnata, Menzies, Swissport, Star Aviation) after Qantas outsourced ground handling in 2020, so three global handler accounts cover most of the ramp. High labor pressure (strikes, $90M Qantas penalty, TWU campaign on pay/hours) plus Master Plan 2045 growth targets make SYD attractive for retrofit autonomy. Note Western Sydney International (separate airport, opening Oct 2026) is launching Australia's first airport-wide pooled electric GSE fleet with Freightquip, a signal Australian ramps are open to new GSE models.

name: São Paulo/Guarulhos–Governor André Franco Montoro International Airport
aerovectNotes: Largest South American hub; Swissport active here.

name: Beijing Daxing International Airport
aerovectNotes: Greenfield mega-hub (opened 2019); modern apron, but access constraints as above.

name: Kempegowda International Airport Bengaluru

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation
groundHandlers.role: Ground handler (passenger, ramp, baggage) across T1 and T2 from Apr 1, 2026 under a 15-year license
groundHandlers.notes: Announced Feb 3, 2026; investing US$9.2M to modernize and standardize its GSE fleet incl. electric GSE, and hiring ~1,000 staff over 3 years on top of its existing 1,700-person BLR cargo team (menziesaviation.com press release)

groundHandlers.name: AISATS (Air India SATS Airport Services)
groundHandlers.role: Ground handling and cargo; operates AISATS Coolport pharma/perishables cold-chain terminal
groundHandlers.notes: Air India + SATS JV; one of the incumbent handlers at BLR (aviationpros.com)

groundHandlers.name: Menzies Aviation Bobba (Bangalore) Pvt Ltd
groundHandlers.role: Cargo terminal operator (JV of Menzies Aviation and Bobba Group)
groundHandlers.notes: Runs one of BLR's three cargo terminals (aviationpros.com)

groundHandlers.name: GlobeGround India
groundHandlers.notes: Listed among BLR ground handling operators (thenetworkplan.com)

groundHandlers.name: Celebi Airport Services India
groundHandlers.role: Former ground handler; exited after India revoked its security clearance May 15, 2025
groundHandlers.notes: Bengaluru was one of nine Indian airports where Celebi operated; other GHAs absorbed operations within days (outlookbusiness.com, maritimegateway.com)

tailwinds.headline: Menzies Aviation secures 15-year ground handling license at BLR (T1+T2) with US$9.2M GSE modernization incl. electric GSE and ~1,000 new hires
tailwinds.relevance: A major global handler standing up a fresh, standardized tractor fleet at BLR from Apr 2026 is a direct retrofit and pilot opportunity, and a wedge into Menzies globally

tailwinds.headline: India revokes Celebi Airport Services India's security clearance; ground handling at nine airports incl. Bengaluru transitioned to other GHAs
tailwinds.relevance: Handler churn reopened contracts and staffing at BLR, increasing appetite for capacity and productivity solutions that reduce dependence on scarce ramp labor

tailwinds.headline: BIAL deploys AI computer-vision Smart Airside Safety System at high-risk apron intersections
tailwinds.relevance: Shows the airport operator is already approving AI systems airside, lowering the regulatory/stakeholder barrier for autonomous tractor trials

operationsNotes: India's third-busiest airport: 41.88M passengers and 502,480 MT cargo in FY2024-25, and a record 532,000 MT cargo in FY2025-26 (+6% YoY). BIAL has an approved ~$2B expansion incl. reserved space for Terminal 3 and an automated people mover, targeting 100M+ annual passengers. BIAL deployed an AI computer-vision Smart Airside Safety System at apron/service-road intersections (reported May 2026), automation-adjacent but not vehicle autonomy.
aerovectNotes: No autonomous-GSE vendor found on this ramp. Strong retrofit window: Menzies begins a 15-year T1/T2 handling license Apr 2026 and is deploying $9.2M of new/modernized GSE (incl. electric), so its BLR fleet decisions are being made now; Menzies is also a global-account door. Celebi's forced May 2025 exit reshuffled the handler landscape, and BIAL is demonstrably receptive to airside AI (Smart Airside Safety System live May 2026). Risk: if Menzies buys new electric tractors rather than retrofitting, the near-term fleet may be young but OEM-uncommitted on autonomy.

name: Munich Airport

groundHandlers.name: AeroGround Flughafen Muenchen GmbH
groundHandlers.role: Primary ramp and baggage handler (airport subsidiary)
groundHandlers.notes: About 2,500 employees handling aircraft, baggage and passenger transport for 100+ airline customers at MUC. Source: https://www.munich-airport.com/aeroground-muenchen-2345761

groundHandlers.name: Swissport Losch Muenchen
groundHandlers.role: Second ramp handler; also passenger services, fueling, baggage
groundHandlers.notes: Signed a cooperation agreement to provide AeroGround ground handling support from March 1, 2024 to secure traffic growth. Source: https://www.swissport.com/en/news/current-news/2024/swissport-losch-and-aeroground-sign-cooperation-agreement

groundHandlers.name: Cargogate Munich Airport GmbH
groundHandlers.notes: Hosts the airside autonomous cargo towing test zone (TractEasy) and earlier evoBOT robot trials. Sources: https://www.munich-airport.com/munich-airport-sets-a-new-benchmark-in-cargo-automation-40269978 and https://www.aviationpros.com/gse/gse-technology/article/53078309/munichs-introduction-to-robotic-cargo-handling

competitors.status: trial: strategic partnership announced Dec 15, 2025; autonomous cargo towing pilot live in a dedicated airside test zone since early 2026, expanded through cargo security gates onto the airfield; roadmap phase covers operational trials, safety validation and scalability planning

tailwinds.headline: Munich Airport partners with TractEasy to advance driverless cargo towing
tailwinds.relevance: Airport leadership has formally committed to an autonomous towing roadmap (trials, safety validation, scalability), validating demand for autonomous GSE at MUC even as a competitor holds the pilot

tailwinds.headline: Munich Airport and Lufthansa join FTE Smart Ramp initiative with 3-5 year autonomous ramp vision
tailwinds.relevance: Joint airport-airline automation program explicitly motivated by staffing shortages; autonomous baggage transport named as the next phase, an opening for retrofit vendors on handler fleets

tailwinds.headline: Munich Airport opens EUR 665M Terminal 1 Pier, expanding apron capacity and ground handling operations
tailwinds.relevance: Up to 6M additional annual passengers and more aircraft stands increase turnaround and baggage-tow volume on a ramp already short on labor

operationsNotes: Handler_led ramp: AeroGround (airport subsidiary) and Swissport Losch split ramp work, with Cargogate on cargo. Terminal 1 Pier opened April 2026 (EUR 665M, +6M annual pax, expanded apron and ground handling operations). Airside autonomous cargo test zone operating since early 2026. Munich Airport and Lufthansa joined the FTE Smart Ramp initiative (March 2026) targeting autonomous freight/baggage transport, remote boarding bridges, virtual geofencing, and FOD robots by 2027, with staffing shortages cited as a driver.
aerovectNotes: MUC has strong autonomy appetite (FTE Smart Ramp member, 3-5 year autonomous ramp vision with Lufthansa) but the towing lane is already contested: TractEasy holds a formal strategic partnership plus a live airside cargo pilot since early 2026 with a scale-up roadmap. Not competitor_held since it is still a pilot, not a deployment. Retrofit opening would be the AeroGround/Swissport Losch baggage tractor fleets, which the cargo-side TractEasy pilot does not cover; baggage is named as the next exploration phase, so timing is tight.

name: Sheremetyevo International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Sheremetyevo Handling LLC
groundHandlers.role: Primary passenger and ramp handler; part of the Sheremetyevo airport shareholders' group; handles Aeroflot PJSC aircraft and passengers. Aeroflot's board reviewed changed deal terms with it in March 2025 (https://www.akm.ru/eng/news/aeroflot-board-of-directors-to-review-maintenance-issues-at-sheremetyevo-airport-on-march-4/)
groundHandlers.notes: Hub carrier Aeroflot Group (Aeroflot, Rossiya, Pobeda) outsources to this airport-affiliated handler rather than self-handling, making the ramp handler_led with a single dominant airline customer.

groundHandlers.name: Moscow Cargo LLC
groundHandlers.role: Main cargo ground handling operator, handles more than 80% of SVO cargo and mail turnover; largest airfreight terminal in Russia/CIS, 380k tonnes/yr design capacity (https://www.moscow-cargo.com/index.php/en/about-us)
groundHandlers.notes: Customers include Aeroflot Group, Volga-Dnepr, Nordwind and Asian/Middle East partner carriers.

groundHandlers.name: A-Group
groundHandlers.role: Dedicated ground handling provider for business aviation at SVO (https://a-group.aero/inside/ground_handling1/)
groundHandlers.notes: Business aviation only, not commercial ramp.

tailwinds.headline: Sheremetyevo completes migration of baggage-handling control to unified domestic Russian software across terminals, replacing Beumer/Alstef/Vanderlande/RESA systems
tailwinds.relevance: Shows SVO is actively investing in ramp/baggage automation at scale, but also confirms Western technology vendors are being displaced by import substitution, closing the door to non-Russian autonomy suppliers.

tailwinds.headline: Pulkovo to deploy fully autonomous fleet of 45 Cognitive Pilot baggage tractors by end of 2026, first in Russia
tailwinds.relevance: First Russian autonomous-GSE fleet proves regulatory and operational feasibility in-country and creates a domestic vendor precedent Sheremetyevo would likely follow; competitive intel rather than an AeroVect opening.

tailwinds.headline: Pulkovo operating company CEO says unmanned ground equipment could save Russian airports up to RUB 70B per year
tailwinds.relevance: Public economic case for autonomous GSE being made at the national level in Russia, driven by labor shortage; signals Russian hubs including SVO are primed for domestic autonomy programs.

operationsNotes: Aeroflot Group hub (Aeroflot, Rossiya, Pobeda). Ramp and passenger handling by airport-affiliated Sheremetyevo Handling LLC; cargo dominated by Moscow Cargo LLC (80%+). Baggage infrastructure is large and automation-heavy: ~29 km of conveyors, a 2 km inter-terminal baggage tunnel under an active runway, up to 20,000 bags/day, control migrated to a single domestic software platform in June 2026 (https://frontierindia.com/russia-replaces-foreign-airport-software-at-sheremetyevo-in-major-tech-shift/, https://www.vedomosti.ru/business/articles/2026/06/16/1206109-sheremetevo-perevelo-upravlenie-bagazhnoi-sistemoi-polnostyu-na-otechestvennii-soft). Airport-specific labor precedent: June 2019 mass resignation of baggage loaders after pay cuts collapsed baggage operations (https://meduza.io/en/feature/2019/06/04/luggage-systems-collapse-at-sheremetyevo). Country labor context: record 2.6M worker shortage at end 2024 with transport/logistics among the worst-hit sectors (https://kyivindependent.com/russia-faces-record-2-6-million-worker-shortage-amid-war-recruitment/, https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2025/07/31/russias-labor-shortage-persists-despite-central-bank-optimism-a90044).
aerovectNotes: Structurally attractive (severe labor shortage, huge Aeroflot hub volume, proven automation appetite) but effectively non-addressable for a Western vendor: Russia sanctions regime, and SVO just completed replacing its Western baggage-system software (Beumer, Alstef, Vanderlande, RESA) with a unified domestic platform as import substitution (https://frontierindia.com/russia-replaces-foreign-airport-software-at-sheremetyevo-in-major-tech-shift/). Domestic vendor Cognitive Pilot is establishing the autonomous-tractor precedent at Pulkovo and would be the natural supplier to SVO. No autonomous-GSE activity found at SVO itself as of July 2026. Track as market intel only.

competitors.status: NOT at SVO. Deploying 45 autonomous baggage tractors at Pulkovo (LED, St. Petersburg) by end of 2026, billed as first in Russia; the likely domestic vendor if Sheremetyevo automates its ramp

name: Istanbul Sabiha Gökçen International Airport

groundHandlers.name: Pegasus Airlines (in-house ground operations)
groundHandlers.role: Carrier self-handler for its own flights
groundHandlers.notes: Pegasus, SAW's dominant carrier, took ramp services in-house (full ramp ops from June 1, 2016), hiring ~1,000 ground staff and spending EUR 7.2M on GSE incl. 38 baggage trucks and 13 pushbacks. Source: https://www.flypgs.com/en/press-room/press-releases/pegasus-expands-its-ground-operations-at-istanbuls-sabiha-gokcen-airport-for-an-even-more-seamless-guest-experience

groundHandlers.name: TGS (Turkish Ground Services)
groundHandlers.notes: JV of Turkish Airlines and Havas (est. Jan 1, 2010); lists Sabiha Gokcen among its stations. Source: https://tgs.aero/en

groundHandlers.name: Celebi Ground Handling Turkey
groundHandlers.notes: Celebi operates a station at Sabiha Gokcen (listed among its Turkish stations); SAW is included in its 10-airport electric GSE program. Sources: https://www.celebiaviation.com/turkey and https://www.ebrd.com/home/news-and-events/news/2025/ebrd-supports-electrification-of-celebi-aviation-s-ground-handli.html

tailwinds.headline: Sabiha Gokcen sets all-time record: 48M passengers and 270,000+ flights in 2025, up from 41.4M in 2024
tailwinds.relevance: Rapid traffic growth (+24% international) strains ramp capacity and staffing, strengthening the case for autonomous baggage tractors.

tailwinds.headline: EBRD lends Celebi Aviation EUR 18M to buy and electrify ground support equipment at 10 Turkish airports including Sabiha Gokcen
